claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit in the UK.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 4 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 4 years old.

Now I need to ask: Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And do you live with Alex, or does he live with someone else?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 4 years old, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for him, you appear to meet the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Just a few more questions to confirm:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex (such as the other parent or another family member)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - my ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's an important detail.

Under the Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any given time. Since your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would not be eligible to claim it as well.

However, if circumstances change and you believe you should be the one receiving Child Benefit instead, you and your ex-partner would need to agree between yourselves who should claim it. If you cannot agree, HMRC will decide who should receive it. The person currently claiming would need to stop their claim, and then you could make a new claim.

Based on the current situation where your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you are not eligible to receive Child Benefit for him at this time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK, which would normally satisfy the basic eligibility criteria. However, under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any given time. The claimant's ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex while the ex-partner's claim remains active. To become eligible, the current claimant (ex-partner) would need to stop their claim first.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 4), who lives with them in the UK. While the claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (responsible for a child under 16, living in the UK, child lives with them), they cannot claim Child Benefit because the ex-partner is already claiming it for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any time.

To become eligible, the current claimant (ex-partner) would need to stop their claim first.

While the claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria (responsible for a child under 16, living in the UK, child lives with them), they cannot claim Child Benefit because the ex-partner is already claiming it for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any time.
